correct_diagnosis.primary: Acute viral gastroenteritis with mild dehydration
required_evidence: Acute onset nausea/vomiting and/or diarrhea with signs of volume depletion (e.g., dry mucous membranes, tachycardia, orthostasis, decreased urine output)
No focal abdominal peritonitis and no red flags suggesting surgical abdomen (e.g., rebound/guarding)
No high-risk features requiring immediate antibiotics/admission (e.g., severe dehydration, persistent hypotension, altered mental status)
Basic labs consistent with dehydration (elevated BUN relative to baseline; electrolytes may show mild abnormalities)

expected_lab_results.WBC: Normal to mildly elevated (stress leukocytosis possible)
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Normal to mildly elevated due to hemoconcentration
expected_lab_results.Sodium: Normal to mildly low (depending on free-water intake) or mildly high with dehydration
expected_lab_results.Potassium: Mildly low possible with vomiting/diarrhea (or normal)
expected_lab_results.Chloride: May be low with vomiting; may be normal/low with diarrhea
expected_lab_results.Bicarbonate (CO2): Mildly low with diarrhea (non-anion gap metabolic acidosis) or mildly high with vomiting (metabolic alkalosis); overall often mildly abnormal

medications: Oral rehydration solution 250-500 mL PO after each loose stool (small frequent sips as tolerated)
Ondansetron ODT 4 mg PO every 8 hours as needed for nausea/vomiting (max 3 doses/day typical outpatient use)
Acetaminophen 650 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for fever/pain (max 3000 mg/day unless otherwise directed)
non_pharmacological: Encourage oral rehydration with electrolyte solution; avoid high-sugar drinks and alcohol
Advance diet as tolerated (bland foods); continue breastfeeding/regular diet in children if applicable
Hand hygiene and infection control; avoid preparing food for others until symptom-free for 48 hours
Return precautions: inability to keep fluids down, worsening dizziness/syncope, blood in stool, severe abdominal pain, persistent fever, signs of dehydration (minimal urination), symptoms >72 hours
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 24-48 hours if not improving; sooner (same day/ED) if red flags or unable to maintain hydration

must_include: Likely self-limited stomach/intestinal infection causing fluid loss
Main risk is dehydration; labs may reflect dehydration
What symptoms would suggest a more serious cause (blood in stool, severe pain, persistent high fever, confusion)
Primary treatment is rehydration (how to use oral rehydration solution and dosing approach)
How to take ondansetron and when to stop/seek care if vomiting persists
Avoid unnecessary antibiotics unless specific features develop (e.g., dysentery, high fever, travel exposure, immunocompromise)
Review allergies (penicillin, shellfish) and confirm no exposure in recommended meds
Discuss medication side effects (ondansetron constipation/headache; rare QT prolongation risk in predisposed patients)
Clarify when to go to ED vs manage at home
Hydration strategy and diet progression
Infection prevention (handwashing, avoid food prep for others until recovered)
Avoid NSAIDs if dehydrated due to kidney risk; use acetaminophen instead if needed

correct_diagnosis.primary: Mucolipidosis (lysosomal storage disorder; likely mucolipidosis type II/III spectrum)
acceptable_alternatives: Lysosomal storage disorder (unspecified) pending confirmatory testing
required_evidence: Clinical phenotype consistent with mucolipidosis (coarse facial features, developmental delay, skeletal dysplasia/contractures, short stature, recurrent respiratory/ear infections, hepatosplenomegaly and/or cardiac involvement)
Disproportionately elevated multiple lysosomal enzyme activities in plasma/serum (suggesting mis-targeting; classically seen in ML II/III)
Confirmatory genetic testing (GNPTAB or GNPTG variants) and/or demonstration of deficient GlcNAc-1-phosphotransferase activity
Radiographic evidence of dysostosis multiplex (if imaging available)
Exclusion of close differentials (e.g., mucopolysaccharidoses: abnormal urinary GAGs; Pompe: CK and cardiomyopathy pattern; congenital hypothyroidism)

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Often normal; may show mild normocytic anemia in chronic disease/inflammation
expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Often normal; may be mildly elevated with intercurrent infection

expected_lab_results.Comprehensive Metabolic Panel - Alkaline: May be elevated in children due to bone turnover; can be above age-adjusted norms with skeletal disease

medications: Acetaminophen 15 mg/kg PO q6h PRN pain/fever (max 75 mg/kg/day; do not exceed adult max if applicable)
Ibuprofen 10 mg/kg PO q6-8h PRN pain/inflammation (avoid if dehydration/renal risk; max 40 mg/kg/day)
non_pharmacological: Referral to genetics/metabolic specialist for confirmatory testing and subtype classification (GNPTAB/GNPTG) and family counseling

Multidisciplinary supportive care: pulmonology (airway clearance plan, sleep study if OSA suspected), cardiology (echo/ECG screening), orthopedics (dysostosis/hip/spine), ENT/audiology (hearing assessment; manage recurrent otitis), ophthalmology (corneal/clouding evaluation), dentistry, PT/OT/speech therapy
Vaccinations up to date; consider influenza/COVID per age and pneumococcal per risk; infection-prevention counseling
Nutritional assessment and feeding/swallow evaluation if aspiration/poor growth; consider reflux management if clinically indicated
Anticipatory guidance: progressive nature, mobility aids, school/early intervention services, and palliative/supportive resources as needed
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Metabolic genetics follow-up within 2-4 weeks (earlier if rapid progression/respiratory issues); multidisciplinary surveillance every 3-6 months depending on severity

must_include: Mucolipidosis is a genetic lysosomal storage disorder affecting how cells process and traffic enzymes, leading to multi-organ involvement
Diagnosis is suspected clinically but requires confirmatory enzyme pattern and genetic testing to define the exact type (II vs III) and guide prognosis
Condition is chronic and often progressive; goals focus on monitoring complications and maximizing function/quality of life
No curative standard therapy for ML II/III; management is supportive and multidisciplinary
Key monitoring targets: breathing/sleep, heart function, hearing/vision, growth/nutrition, bones/joints and mobility
Clear plan for symptom control (pain/fever regimen) and when to seek urgent care (respiratory distress, dehydration, severe pain, new neurologic symptoms)

Review expected disease course variability by subtype and individual; set realistic expectations and supportive resources

Safe activity guidance tailored to orthopedic limitations; emphasize PT/OT and fall prevention

Discuss prognosis variability and what 'regression' means in this condition

Arrange follow-up for monitoring response/side effects and coordination (neurology, genetics, PT/OT/speech, nutrition, orthopedics; timeframe based on treatment initiation and symptom severity).

correct_diagnosis.primary: Familial lipoprotein lipase deficiency (familial chylomicronemia syndrome, Type I hyperlipoproteinemia)
acceptable_alternatives: Apolipoprotein C-II deficiency (phenocopy of LPL deficiency)
required_evidence: Marked fasting hypertriglyceridemia typically > 1000 mg/dL (often > 2000 mg/dL) with recurrent/persistent elevation
Lipoprotein electrophoresis showing predominant chylomicrons (Type I pattern)
Low/absent post-heparin plasma lipoprotein lipase activity supporting LPL pathway defect
Apolipoprotein B low/normal (helps distinguish from polygenic/apoB-driven hypertriglyceridemia)
Clinical features consistent with chylomicronemia (e.g., splenomegaly; may also include eruptive xanthomas, lipemia retinalis, pancreatitis history)

expected_lab_results.Triglycerides (fasting): Severely elevated, typically >1000 mg/dL (often >2000 mg/dL); sample may appear lipemic
expected_lab_results.Total cholesterol: Normal to moderately elevated; often less elevated than expected relative to triglycerides
expected_lab_results.Non-HDL cholesterol: Normal to mildly elevated; not markedly elevated compared with triglycerides
expected_lab_results.Apolipoprotein B: Low to normal (often <75 mg/dL), suggesting low number of apoB-containing particles
expected_lab_results.Lipoprotein electrophoresis: Predominant chylomicrons with minimal VLDL/LDL (Type I hyperlipoproteinemia pattern)
expected_lab_results.Lipoprotein lipase activity (post-hepari: Markedly reduced or absent LPL activity

non_pharmacological: Very-low-fat diet: target <10–15% of total calories from fat (often ≤20 g/day), avoid long-chain triglycerides
Use medium-chain triglycerides (MCT) as needed for calories (do not require chylomicron transport)
Strict avoidance of alcohol
Avoid simple/refined carbohydrates and fructose-heavy intake (to reduce VLDL production even if chylomicrons predominate)
Weight optimization and regular physical activity as tolerated

Avoid secondary contributors: uncontrolled diabetes, hypothyroidism, pregnancy-related worsening, and triglyceride-raising drugs (e.g., estrogens, isotretinoin, atypical antipsychotics, protease inhibitors, thiazides, beta-blockers in some cases)
Pancreatitis risk mitigation: counsel on urgent evaluation for severe abdominal pain, nausea/vomiting; low threshold for emergency care when TG very high

Alcohol avoidance is essential due to triglyceride and pancreatitis risk

Plan for monitoring triglycerides and adjusting diet with a dietitian experienced in FCS
Specialist referral for genetics and consideration of targeted therapies where available

Set expectations about chronic management and the importance of adherence to prevent pancreatitis

Perform required drug interaction check prior to prescribing any medication (e.g., omega-3 fatty acids, pain meds if needed) and review current meds/supplements.

Arrange close follow-up for TG monitoring, dietitian experienced in FCS, and specialist referral (lipidology/endocrinology/genetics); set monitoring intervals and escalation plan for symptoms.

correct_diagnosis.primary: LACC1-associated juvenile idiopathic arthritis (monogenic juvenile arthritis due to LACC1 defect)
acceptable_alternatives: Autoinflammatory syndrome with chronic arthritis (monogenic autoinflammatory arthritis)
required_evidence: Childhood-onset chronic inflammatory arthritis (persistent joint swelling/pain and morning stiffness >6 weeks)

Negative or non-supportive autoantibodies for classic autoimmune disease (e.g., ANA negative or low-titer, no other lupus features)
CBC pattern consistent with inflammation (anemia of chronic disease and/or thrombocytosis possible)
Ferritin may be elevated as an acute-phase reactant (marked hyperferritinemia raises concern for macrophage activation syndrome if systemic symptoms present)
Known pathogenic LACC1 variant or strong clinical suspicion of monogenic JIA phenotype (familial/early onset/refractory course)

expected_lab_results.CBC - MCV: Normal (normocytic) or mildly low if concurrent iron deficiency; typically normocytic in inflammation

correct_diagnosis.primary: Neonatal-onset inflammatory bowel disease with inflammatory dermatitis (very-early-onset IBD phenotype; consider monogenic immune dysregulation such as IL10/IL10R pathway defect)
acceptable_alternatives: Monogenic immunodeficiency/immune dysregulation presenting as neonatal enterocolitis and dermatitis (e.g., IL10RA/IL10RB deficiency, IPEX)

required_evidence: Onset in neonatal period/early infancy with severe or persistent diarrhea and blood/mucus in stool or failure to thrive
Inflammatory skin findings (e.g., eczematous/psoriasiform dermatitis, perianal dermatitis, folliculitis-like lesions) concurrent with GI symptoms
Elevated inflammatory markers (CRP and/or ESR) and/or leukocytosis
Hypoalbuminemia suggesting protein-losing enteropathy or significant intestinal inflammation
Exclusion of common infectious etiologies and consideration of immunodeficiency/immune dysregulation (history of recurrent/severe infections, poor growth, family history/consanguinity)

non_pharmacological: Urgent pediatric gastroenterology and immunology referral for evaluation of monogenic VEO-IBD/immune dysregulation (including stool studies for infection, immune workup, and genetic testing as indicated)

Skin care: gentle fragrance-free emollients at least twice daily; avoid irritants and harsh soaps

Avoid empiric antibiotics unless infection is suspected/confirmed; if amoebiasis is a concern based on exposure/travel, confirm with stool testing and treat per pediatric infectious disease guidance
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Within 24-72 hours for clinical reassessment (sooner/ED immediately if worsening, dehydration, fever, lethargy, or increased blood in stool); specialist follow-up urgently (within 1 week or sooner based on severity)

must_include: Symptoms suggest significant intestinal inflammation plus skin inflammation; in very young infants this can be a special form of IBD related to immune system problems

Skin care routine and avoidance of irritants; nickel avoidance for contact dermatitis history

minimal_paths.description: Low-complexity safe path: recognize suspected neonatal inflammatory skin+bowel disease, assess acuity, order initial workup, provide supportive education, and arrange urgent specialist evaluation without starting antibiotics/immunosuppression.

ORDER_LAB: CBC with differential, CMP/electrolytes, CRP/ESR, stool occult blood, stool culture/PCR as locally available, C. difficile testing only if appropriate for age/risk, fecal calprotectin (interpret cautiously in infants), urinalysis if dehydration concern; consider immunology screen (Ig levels, lymphocyte subsets) per local pathways
GET_RESULTS: identify dehydration/electrolyte abnormalities, anemia, leukocytosis, elevated inflammatory markers, stool inflammatory/infectious signals
DIAGNOSE: working diagnosis of significant intestinal + skin inflammation; discuss differential including infection, allergic proctocolitis, very-early-onset IBD/monogenic immune disorders

EDUCATE: explain diagnosis uncertainty and need for stool tests/imaging/endoscopy as needed plus immune/genetic evaluation; red flags; hydration/feeding guidance; skin barrier care; avoid empiric antibiotics/immunosuppression; infection prevention

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 24-year-old anxious but clear communicator. Start with: “I keep having weird episodes where my vision goes funny and I blank out for a minute.” Volunteer: blurry edges or flashing zig-zag shapes for seconds, brief staring/unresponsiveness, confusion after, headaches twice weekly in the back of the head, bumping into things/poor side awareness. If asked, add: partner notices chewing/lip-smacking, post-episode fatigue, triggers (bright lights, lack of sleep), reading lines jumble/lose place, misjudging reach. Do not mention unless specifically explored: lifelong clumsiness/hand-eye issues, disorientation in busy visual places, longstanding learning problems (math/maps/copying), occasional object recognition difficulty, brief missing parts of vision. Be resistant to disclose: one episode of urinary incontinence, avoiding driving, losing time at work, fear of being seen as “crazy”; reveal only after trust/empathy. Express misconceptions: “Maybe it’s migraine aura/panic/low blood sugar/just my glasses.” If clinician is empathetic, you relax, admit fears about costs, insurance/job, surprise bills, and doubts about generics.

Sertraline 50 mg PO daily
Norethindrone/ethinyl estradiol 1 mg/20 mcg PO daily
Cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 2000 IU PO daily
comorbidities: Focal epilepsy secondary to bilateral parasagittal parieto-occipital polymicrogyria
confounders: Migraine with visual aura causing transient visual symptoms that can mimic occipital seizures
SSRI use (sertraline) and sleep deprivation increasing seizure susceptibility and complicating symptom attribution
Oxcarbazepine-associated hyponatremia contributing to dizziness/confusion and potentially lowering seizure threshold
Combined oral contraceptive reduced efficacy due to oxcarbazepine enzyme induction (risk of unintended pregnancy affecting antiseizure medication decisions)
Intermittent cannabis edible use for sleep (may alter cognition and seizure threshold, complicating history)

correct_diagnosis.primary: Bilateral parasagittal parieto-occipital polymicrogyria (cortical malformation) with epilepsy risk (focal seizures with possible secondary generalization)
acceptable_alternatives: Structural focal epilepsy due to cortical malformation (polymicrogyria)
required_evidence: Brain MRI demonstrating bilateral parasagittal parieto-occipital polymicrogyria (irregular cortical thickening with excessive small gyri and abnormal gray-white junction)
Clinical history consistent with seizures and/or developmental/visual-spatial deficits attributable to parieto-occipital involvement
Exclusion of acute metabolic derangement on basic labs that could better explain events (e.g., severe hyponatremia)
EEG (if available) showing focal epileptiform discharges consistent with posterior quadrant/parieto-occipital onset (supportive but not mandatory if MRI and clinical picture are clear)

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Typically normal (no specific abnormality expected from polymicrogyria)
expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Typically normal (no specific abnormality expected unless intercurrent infection)
expected_lab_results.CMP - Sodium: Typically normal; ensure not significantly low prior to initiating sodium-channel antiepileptics or if using oxcarbazepine (risk of hyponatremia)
expected_lab_results.CMP - Bicarbonate (CO2): Typically normal; consider baseline if using topiramate/zonisamide (metabolic acidosis risk)
expected_lab_results.CMP - BUN: Typically normal; assess renal function surrogate before renally-cleared antiseizure meds (e.g., levetiracetam)

medications: Levetiracetam 500 mg PO twice daily (titrate by 500 mg/day every 1-2 weeks as tolerated to typical target 1000-1500 mg PO twice daily)

Midazolam (intranasal) 5 mg in one nostril for seizure lasting >5 minutes; may repeat once after 10 minutes if still seizing (max 10 mg) OR Diazepam rectal gel 0.2 mg/kg PR once for prolonged seizure per local protocol
non_pharmacological: Seizure safety counseling: no driving until seizure-free per jurisdiction; avoid swimming/bathing alone; heights/heavy machinery precautions
Trigger management: sleep regularity, avoid alcohol/recreational drugs, medication adherence strategies

Neurology referral for epilepsy management; consider epilepsy surgery evaluation only if drug-resistant epilepsy (failure of 2 appropriate antiseizure medications) and seizures are localizable; note bilateral polymicrogyria often limits resective options but neuromodulation (VNS/RNS/DBS) may be considered in specialized centers
EEG and updated MRI review (if not recent); ophthalmology/visual field assessment if symptoms suggest occipital involvement; neuropsychology evaluation if cognitive/learning concerns
Genetic counseling/testing consideration (polymicrogyria can be syndromic) and assessment for associated comorbidities (developmental delay, motor deficits)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Neurology follow-up in 2-4 weeks after initiation/titration (earlier if adverse effects or breakthrough seizures); urgent evaluation for status epilepticus, new focal deficits, or significant change in seizure pattern

must_include: Polymicrogyria is a congenital malformation of brain cortex development (not contagious; not caused by anything the patient did)
Location (bilateral parasagittal parieto-occipital) can predispose to seizures and sometimes visual-spatial/cognitive symptoms
Many patients develop epilepsy; goal is seizure control and safety; prognosis varies
Rationale for antiseizure medication: reduce seizure frequency/severity and prevent injury
Why levetiracetam is chosen: effective for focal seizures, minimal interactions, no cross-reactivity with lamotrigine rash history
How to take medication and titration plan; importance of adherence and not stopping abruptly
Rescue plan for prolonged seizures and when to call emergency services
Review allergies explicitly: avoid lamotrigine due to prior rash; document penicillin urticaria history (not directly relevant to antiseizure meds but important for records)
Common side effects of levetiracetam (irritability, mood changes, somnolence) and when to report urgently (severe mood changes/suicidal ideation, rash though uncommon)
Discuss reproductive considerations if applicable (teratogenicity counseling; folic acid if of childbearing potential; coordinate with neurology/OB for pregnancy planning)
Seizure first aid for family/caregivers (protect from injury, place on side, time the seizure, do not restrain, nothing in mouth)
Activity restrictions and legal driving guidance until cleared
Sleep hygiene, stress management, avoidance of alcohol/recreational drugs, and medication adherence tools

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 34-year-old female accounting specialist. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Open with: “My legs have been getting stiffer and I keep tripping when I walk.” Early in the visit, volunteer: legs feel tight/stiff (worse when walking faster), toes catch the ground, stairs are harder, balance worse with a couple near-falls. If asked about timeline, say it’s slowly worsened over a few years; stiffness is worse after sitting and takes a minute to get going; occasional nighttime calf cramps; you can’t lift the front of your feet well and drag them. If asked about bladder, share urgency and sometimes barely making it. Do not mention unless specifically asked: shoes wearing unevenly/outer edges, worse when tired/stressed, avoiding long walks due to fear of tripping. Resist/feel embarrassed: minor urine leakage and sex being less satisfying due to tight legs—share only if directly asked, and minimize at first. If asked about other symptoms, mention heavy eyelids/eye fatigue and end-of-day “worn out” weakness. Express concerns: PT and genetic testing cost/coverage, and fear a genetic diagnosis will raise life insurance premiums. If the clinician shows empathy, acknowledge it, relax slightly, and answer more openly.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Hereditary spastic paraplegia
acceptable_alternatives: Primary lateral sclerosis (if adult-onset with UMN-only features and no family history)
required_evidence: Progressive, predominantly lower-extremity spasticity with upper motor neuron signs (hyperreflexia, clonus, extensor plantar responses)
Absence of prominent fatigable weakness, ptosis/ophthalmoplegia, bulbar symptoms (argues against postsynaptic congenital myasthenic syndrome)
Absence of primary myopathic features (proximal weakness out of proportion to spasticity, muscle wasting, markedly elevated CK, myopathic EMG) suggesting myofibrillar myopathy
Normal or non-contributory basic labs (CBC/CMP) without metabolic/toxic explanation
Supportive history such as family history and/or insidious onset with slow progression

medications: Baclofen 5 mg PO three times daily, titrate every 3-7 days as tolerated toward 10-20 mg PO three times daily for spasticity
Acetaminophen 650 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for pain (max 3000 mg/day)
non_pharmacological: Referral to physical therapy for stretching program, gait training, and strengthening; daily home stretching to reduce spasticity
Assistive device assessment (cane/walker/ankle-foot orthoses) and fall-risk mitigation at home
Occupational therapy for mobility and activities of daily living strategies
Genetic counseling and neurology follow-up for confirmation and subtype-specific testing as appropriate
Screen for and manage complications: urinary urgency, constipation, neuropathic pain, mood symptoms
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 4-6 weeks to evaluate spasticity control, side effects, and functional status; earlier (1-2 weeks) if sedation, weakness, or falls occur

must_include: Condition affects upper motor neuron pathways causing stiffness and spasms mainly in the legs
Typically slowly progressive; many people maintain independence with therapy and symptom control
Why alternatives are less likely (no fatigable weakness/ocular-bulbar signs for myasthenic syndrome; no primary muscle disease pattern for myofibrillar myopathy)
Plan for confirmation/characterization (neurology evaluation; consider genetic testing; consider MRI/EMG if clinically indicated)
Goal is symptom control (reduce spasticity, improve gait/function) and prevent complications; not a rapid cure
How to take baclofen and that it will be titrated slowly
Common side effects (sleepiness, dizziness, weakness) and safety precautions (avoid driving/alcohol until effects known)
Do not stop baclofen abruptly; contact clinician for tapering if needed
Discuss fall risk and when to seek urgent care (sudden new weakness, severe worsening, new bowel/bladder retention, new numbness, severe back pain)
Invite questions about prognosis, inheritance/family planning, and work/school accommodations
Confirm penicillin allergy details and ensure it is documented
Regular stretching/strengthening and consistent PT participation
Home safety (remove tripping hazards, supportive footwear, consider grab bars)
Maintain activity within tolerance; avoid prolonged immobility which can worsen stiffness

nl_assertions: Agent elicits a coherent spasticity-focused history: slowly progressive bilateral leg stiffness/spasms, gait difficulty, hyperreflexia/UMN symptoms, triggers, functional limitations, and family history suggestive of inheritance.

Agent screens for red flags requiring urgent evaluation: sudden new weakness, severe/worsening symptoms, new bowel/bladder retention, new numbness/sensory level, severe back pain, fever/weight loss, or acute trauma.

Agent explicitly explains HSP as an upper motor neuron pathway disorder causing leg stiffness/spasms and emphasizes typically slow progression and that many maintain independence with therapy and symptom control.

Agent contrasts key alternatives per milestone: states absence of fatigable weakness/ocular-bulbar signs makes myasthenic syndrome less likely, and absence of primary muscle disease pattern makes myofibrillar myopathy less likely.

Before prescribing, agent performs contraindication assessment for baclofen (e.g., hypersensitivity, significant renal impairment/need for dose adjustment, baseline sedation/fall risk, pregnancy/breastfeeding considerations) and addresses concerns or adjusts plan.

Before prescribing, agent checks for drug interactions and additive CNS depression (alcohol, opioids, benzodiazepines, sedatives) and counsels accordingly; omission is non-critical but should be present for full credit.

Agent prescribes baclofen with clear instructions that it is titrated slowly and tailored to response; includes counseling on common side effects (sleepiness, dizziness, weakness) and safety precautions (avoid driving/alcohol until effects known).
Agent warns not to stop baclofen abruptly and instructs to contact clinician for tapering if needed; mentions potential withdrawal risks conceptually (e.g., rebound spasticity).
Agent provides lifestyle guidance: regular stretching/strengthening, consistent PT, maintain activity within tolerance, avoid prolonged immobility, and home safety modifications to reduce falls.

You speak vaguely and are a bit evasive, minimizing symptoms because you worry about costs and missing school or helping at the family convenience store. Start with: “My joints keep swelling up and hurting, and it’s getting harder to walk and use my hands.” If asked “tell me more,” volunteer: morning knee/ankle puffiness and pain, long morning stiffness, wrist/finger pain making writing/opening bottles hard, constant tiredness, missing school/sports due to flare-ups. diagnosis.primary: juvenile arthritis due to defect in LACC1

correct_diagnosis.primary: Juvenile idiopathic arthritis (monogenic autoinflammatory arthritis) due to LACC1 (FAMIN) defect
acceptable_alternatives: Monogenic juvenile arthritis due to LACC1 (FAMIN) mutation
Early-onset inflammatory arthritis/autoinflammatory arthritis associated with LACC1
Polyarticular juvenile idiopathic arthritis (phenotypic label if genetics not yet confirmed)
required_evidence: Childhood-onset chronic inflammatory arthritis (persistent joint swelling/limited range of motion for ≥6 weeks) with recurrent flares
Elevated inflammatory markers during active disease (ESR and/or CRP)
Exclusion of septic arthritis and malignancy based on clinical course and basic labs (no profound leukopenia/thrombocytopenia suggesting marrow failure; no acute toxic presentation)
Genetic confirmation of pathogenic biallelic LACC1 variant (or strong prior documentation of LACC1 defect)
Supportive pattern: systemic inflammation with anemia of chronic disease and/or hyperferritinemia during flares

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Low to mildly low (normocytic or mildly microcytic anemia consistent with inflammation; may worsen with active disease)
expected_lab_results.CBC - MCV: Normal or mildly low (normocytic or borderline microcytic)
expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Normal to elevated (inflammatory leukocytosis possible during flares)
expected_lab_results.CBC - Platelets: Normal to elevated (reactive thrombocytosis common with inflammation)
expected_lab_results.Ferritin: Normal to elevated (acute phase reactant; may be high during flares)
expected_lab_results.Serum iron: Low (consistent with anemia of inflammation; low transferrin saturation expected if measured)

medications: Naproxen 10 mg/kg/day PO divided BID (max 1000 mg/day) for pain/inflammation if no renal/GI contraindications
Methotrexate 10-15 mg/m^2 PO or SC once weekly (max 25 mg/week) with folic acid 1 mg PO daily (or 5 mg once weekly the day after MTX)
Adalimumab 24 mg/m^2 (max 40 mg) SC every 2 weeks if inadequate response to MTX/NSAID or moderate-severe disease; use an alternative TNF inhibitor if formulary requires
Prednisone 0.5 mg/kg/day PO (short course, taper over 2-4 weeks) only as bridge therapy for severe flare; prefer intra-articular corticosteroid injection for limited-joint flares when feasible
non_pharmacological: Pediatric rheumatology co-management; confirm/obtain documentation of LACC1 mutation and prior treatment response
Physical and occupational therapy to preserve range of motion, strength, and function; home exercise plan
Screen for and refer for ophthalmology slit-lamp exam for uveitis per JIA schedule (frequency based on risk factors; ensure baseline exam if not done)
Vaccination review: avoid live vaccines while on biologics/high-dose steroids; ensure inactivated vaccines up to date (including influenza annually)
School/activity accommodations during flares; encourage low-impact activity as tolerated
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess disease activity and labs in 4-6 weeks after initiating/adjusting DMARD; sooner (1-2 weeks) if starting systemic steroids or if symptoms worsen; routine monitoring every 8-12 weeks once stable

must_include: This is a chronic inflammatory arthritis starting in childhood; LACC1 defect can drive persistent immune-mediated joint inflammation
Goals are to control inflammation early to prevent joint damage and preserve function
Flares can raise ESR/CRP and cause anemia of inflammation; labs help track disease activity
Stepwise plan: NSAID for symptoms, methotrexate as a disease-modifying therapy, and biologic therapy if control is inadequate
Expected timeline: symptom improvement may take weeks; methotrexate/biologics are not immediate pain medicines
Key risks and monitoring: infection risk with immunosuppressants; need for periodic CBC and inflammatory markers; avoid live vaccines while on biologics/high-dose steroids
Steroids are short-term bridge only due to side effects; emphasize steroid-sparing strategy
Discuss infection warning signs and when to seek urgent care (fever, severe joint pain with inability to bear weight, rapidly worsening swelling)
Review known allergies (amoxicillin-clavulanate urticarial rash; latex contact dermatitis) and avoidance strategies in clinic/procedures
Shared decision-making about route (oral vs injection), adherence, and family preferences; discuss fertility/pregnancy counseling for adolescents if applicable (methotrexate teratogenicity)
Importance of PT/OT and regular low-impact activity to maintain mobility
Pain management strategies (heat/cold, pacing, sleep hygiene) alongside medication
School and psychosocial support; encourage normal activities with accommodations during flares

Order baseline and activity/monitoring labs typical for inflammatory arthritis and DMARD initiation/monitoring (CBC with differential, CMP/LFTs, ESR/CRP; consider hepatitis B/C and TB screening prior to biologic; pregnancy test for post-menarche patients before methotrexate; consider ferritin/iron studies if anemia suspected).
Review results to support inflammatory activity (ESR/CRP), detect anemia of inflammation, and ensure baseline safety for immunosuppressive therapy (CBC/LFTs).

Synthesize history, exam context (if provided), and labs to confirm juvenile-onset chronic inflammatory arthritis consistent with LACC1-related autoinflammatory/immune-mediated arthritis and to rule out urgent alternate diagnoses when red flags are present.
Perform required allergy check before any prescribing; confirm details of known allergies (amoxicillin-clavulanate urticarial rash; latex contact dermatitis) and implications for meds and procedures.
Perform required drug interaction check before prescribing NSAID, methotrexate, steroids, or biologics, including interactions with OTC meds and supplements.
Re-check interactions if multiple new agents are prescribed or if a biologic is added, ensuring safe combination therapy and avoiding duplicative immunosuppression risks.

Initiate stepwise therapy: NSAID for symptom control; methotrexate as DMARD (with folic acid) if indicated; consider short-term low-dose steroid bridge only if needed; plan escalation to biologic if inadequate control after appropriate trial; include monitoring plan and infection precautions.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment timeline/monitoring, infection warning signs/urgent care triggers, allergy/latex avoidance, shared decision-making on route/adherence, pregnancy prevention counseling for methotrexate as applicable, and lifestyle/PT/OT/school support.

Arrange follow-up to assess response and adverse effects (typically 4–8 weeks after DMARD start or sooner if severe), with lab monitoring cadence (CBC/LFTs/ESR/CRP) and plan for escalation to biologic if targets not met.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety-net instructions, and follow-up plan.

general_concerns: Thinks Waldenstrom macroglobulinemia is 'basically multiple myeloma' and assumes it always needs immediate chemotherapy no matter what symptoms are present.
Believes that if he feels 'mostly fine,' the disease must be gone and follow-up labs are optional.
Assumes fatigue and occasional numbness are just normal aging and not related to the condition, so he downplays symptoms unless directly asked.
Thinks high protein levels in blood mean he should avoid all dietary protein to 'starve the cancer.'
Believes bone marrow biopsy or CT scans can 'spread cancer' and prefers to avoid testing.
Thinks blood viscosity problems can be fixed by drinking more water alone and doesn’t understand why urgent evaluation might be needed for headaches/vision changes.
economic_concerns: Assumes any cancer treatment will be financially catastrophic and avoids discussing insurance details to prevent being 'pressured' into expensive therapy.
Believes generic supplements are a cheaper substitute for prescription therapy and monitoring.
Worries that frequent lab monitoring is a 'money grab' and tries to space out appointments on his own to save copays and time off from his part-time job.

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 71-year-old retired auto body shop owner who now does part-time deliveries. You speak vaguely and are a bit evasive, minimizing symptoms unless asked directly. Start with: for a couple months you’ve been wiped out, lightheaded and blurry-eyed, worse when standing or after a hot shower. If the clinician asks open-endedly about other issues, volunteer: constant fatigue even after sleep, dizziness on standing, intermittent blurry vision, more headaches, and shortness of breath on stairs.
Only if asked specific questions, mention: nosebleeds that take longer to stop, gum bleeding, easy bruising, tingling/numb feet, cold/pale/blue hands/feet in cold, fevers/night sweats, 10-lb unintentional weight loss, and lingering infections.
Do not mention foamy urine, ankle swelling, ringing/muffled hearing, painful cold fingers, or brain fog unless asked about urine/swelling/hearing/cold sensitivity/memory.
Resist bringing up erectile problems and increased nightly alcohol unless directly asked; be embarrassed.
Express misconceptions: blame BP meds for dizziness, glasses/screens for vision, worry about diabetes for tingling, call cold hands “bad circulation.” Be wary of costs, avoid insurance details, prefer supplements, and doubt frequent labs. If met with empathy, soften slightly but stay guarded.

medications: lisinopril 20 mg PO daily
amlodipine 5 mg PO daily
furosemide 20 mg PO daily
atorvastatin 20 mg PO nightly
comorbidities: Autosomal dominant progressive nephropathy with chronic kidney disease (stage 3b)
confounders: Chronic kidney disease with proteinuria (can contribute to anemia and elevated beta-2 microglobulin, complicating staging/attribution of symptoms)
ACE inhibitor therapy with baseline hyperkalemia risk (limits supportive care options and complicates interpretation of electrolyte abnormalities)
Cryoglobulinemia (can cause neuropathy, purpura, and renal findings that may mimic vasculitis or other glomerulopathies)
Anemia of chronic disease/CKD as an alternative contributor to fatigue and dyspnea
Hyperviscosity-related symptoms (headache, blurry vision) may be intermittent and mistaken for uncontrolled hypertension or medication effects

correct_diagnosis.primary: Waldenstrom macroglobulinemia (lymphoplasmacytic lymphoma with IgM paraproteinemia)
acceptable_alternatives: Lymphoplasmacytic lymphoma with IgM monoclonal gammopathy
required_evidence: Unexplained normocytic or mildly macrocytic anemia with elevated ESR and elevated total protein with low/normal albumin (protein gap)
Clinical features attributable to IgM paraprotein (e.g., hyperviscosity symptoms, neuropathy, cryoglobulinemia/cold agglutinin symptoms) or marrow infiltration (fatigue, cytopenias)
Confirmation plan: serum protein electrophoresis with immunofixation showing IgM monoclonal protein and quantitative IgM level
Confirmation plan: bone marrow biopsy demonstrating lymphoplasmacytic lymphoma (with flow cytometry) and MYD88 L265P mutation testing (supportive)

expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Low (anemia, typically moderate; often normocytic or mildly macrocytic)
expected_lab_results.MCV: Normal to mildly high (normocytic or mild macrocytosis)
expected_lab_results.WBC: Normal or mildly low (may be decreased if marrow involvement/therapy-related)
expected_lab_results.Platelets: Normal or low (thrombocytopenia possible with marrow involvement)
expected_lab_results.Albumin: Normal or low (creating an increased protein gap)

medications: Rituximab 375 mg/m^2 IV weekly x4 (or per protocol) (avoid as sole initial therapy if symptomatic hyperviscosity; monitor for IgM flare)
Bendamustine 90 mg/m^2 IV days 1-2 every 28 days x6 cycles (in combination with rituximab, per hematology protocol)
Acyclovir 400 mg PO twice daily (HSV/VZV prophylaxis if receiving anti-CD20/chemo per institutional protocol)
Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole 800/160 mg PO three times weekly (PJP prophylaxis if regimen/steroid exposure warrants per protocol)
non_pharmacological: Urgent therapeutic plasmapheresis if symptomatic hyperviscosity is present or IgM is very high; initiate before rituximab when hyperviscosity suspected
Avoid RBC transfusion until after plasmapheresis if hyperviscosity suspected (transfusion can worsen viscosity); coordinate with hematology/transfusion medicine
Baseline and ongoing monitoring plan: CBC, CMP, quantitative IgM, SPEP/IFE; assess for neuropathy, bleeding, visual changes, headaches, mucosal bleeding
Vaccinations prior to immunochemotherapy when feasible (influenza, pneumococcal, COVID-19); avoid live vaccines during/after therapy per immunosuppression guidance
Hematology/oncology referral for definitive staging and regimen selection; consider BTK inhibitor-based therapy (e.g., zanubrutinib) as an alternative depending on comorbidities and genotype
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Hematology evaluation urgently (within 1 week; same-day if hyperviscosity symptoms). Recheck CBC and IgM within 2-4 weeks after therapy initiation or sooner if symptomatic.

must_include: WM is a slow-growing blood/lymph marrow cancer that makes an abnormal IgM protein
The IgM protein can thicken blood (hyperviscosity) and contribute to fatigue/anemia and other symptoms
Diagnosis requires confirmatory tests (SPEP/IFE, quantitative IgM, bone marrow biopsy) beyond the screening labs
Treatment is guided by symptoms and organ effects; not everyone needs immediate therapy if asymptomatic
If hyperviscosity is suspected, plasmapheresis is urgent and is done before/alongside cancer-directed therapy
Rituximab-based therapy is common; discuss infusion reactions and the possibility of an IgM flare early in treatment
Discuss infection risk and need for prophylaxis/vaccines and prompt evaluation for fever
Red-flag symptoms requiring urgent care: new/worsening vision changes, severe headache, confusion, chest pain, shortness of breath, mucosal bleeding
Shared decision-making about regimen options (chemoimmunotherapy vs BTK inhibitor) based on goals, comorbidities, and side-effect profiles
Avoid NSAIDs/aspirin unless directed if thrombocytopenic or bleeding; review all OTC/supplements
Hydration and avoiding cold exposure if cold agglutinin/cryoglobulin symptoms are present
Plan for regular monitoring and keeping follow-up appointments/labs

minimal_paths.description: Suspected/confirmed WM with symptoms concerning for hyperviscosity; prioritize emergency management and definitive workup.
